items.q: What are James's four characteristics of mystical experience?
items.correct: Ineffability (beyond words), noetic quality (gives knowledge), transience (temporary), and passivity (feeling acted upon by something greater).
items.misconception: Religious experiences are just hallucinations caused by stress.
items.random_wrong: Religious experiences only happen during dreams.
items.why: James was a psychologist describing common features, not assessing truth. Students collapse the question of describing experience into the question of whether God caused it — distinct issues.

items.q: What is the problem of evil?
items.correct: If God is all-loving, all-knowing and all-powerful, why does evil and suffering exist? Logically, all four cannot be true together without explanation.
items.misconception: It is the question of whether evil people will go to hell.
items.random_wrong: It is the question of whether the devil really exists.
items.why: Students treat it as a moral or eschatological question. It is actually a logical challenge to classical theism — and the central problem theodicies try to solve.

items.q: What was the Augustine vs Pelagius dispute about original sin?
items.correct: Augustine argued humans inherit a corrupted nature from Adam (original sin) and cannot achieve salvation without grace; Pelagius argued humans retain free will to choose good without divine intervention and that sin is imitated, not inherited.
items.misconception: Original sin means Christians believe babies are evil.
items.random_wrong: Pelagius and Augustine agreed humans are born innocent.
items.why: Original sin is about inherited nature/condition, not personal guilt of infants. The Pelagian controversy shaped Western Christianity — Augustine won, but the question of how far human nature is corrupted remains theologically contested.

items.q: Does the verification principle pass its own test?
items.correct: Critics argue it does not: the principle is neither analytic nor empirically verifiable, so by its own criterion it would be meaningless — a famous self-refutation problem.
items.misconception: The verification principle is itself empirically verifiable, which is why philosophers accepted it.
items.random_wrong: The verification principle is true by definition, like 'all bachelors are unmarried'.
items.why: Ayer tried to treat the principle as a definition or methodological convention, but the self-application problem stuck and contributed to logical positivism's decline. Students often miss this evaluation point.

items.q: Did Ayer claim that 'God exists' is false?
items.correct: No — Ayer claimed it is factually meaningless: neither true nor false, because unverifiable. By the same token, he held atheism's denial is equally meaningless.
items.misconception: Ayer argued that 'God exists' is false and that atheism is therefore true.
items.random_wrong: Ayer argued that 'God exists' is true but unprovable.
items.why: This is the crucial distinction: Ayer's position is not atheism but non-cognitivism about religious assertions — even 'God does not exist' fails the test. Students routinely conflate 'meaningless' with 'false'.

items.q: Who introduced logical positivism to British philosophy in 1936?
items.why: It's A. J. Ayer. “Bertrand Russell” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: By virtue of its meaning. “By empirical observation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Ayer's Language, Truth and Logic published?
items.why: The real figure is 1936. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
